summary: Joel announces the shipping of CityDesk 2.0 as a free upgrade and explains the decision to eliminate the Home Edition. The two-tier pricing experiment (Home and Professional editions) failed because the cheaper version was too cheap to support profitably and frustrated users with artificial limitations. Fog Creek is absorbing the cost of upgrading all Home Edition users to Professional, lowering the Professional price to $299, and introducing a Contributor Edition for team members who only maintain sites. He also promotes demo videos made with Camtasia Studio.
key_points: The Home Edition / Professional Edition split was a failed experiment in tiered pricing that created more problems than it solved
Selling a crippled product at an unsustainably low price is worse than not offering a cheap option at all
Rather than abandoning cheap-tier customers, Fog Creek chose to absorb the cost and upgrade them all for free
A role-based edition (Contributor) that matches actual usage patterns replaced the artificial feature-limited edition
money_quotes: That was a brave experiment, which was, I have to admit, something of a failure. Call it the Fog Creek PCjr.
We wanted to make a cheaper entry-level version of CityDesk, but in the end we made something that was too cheap to support profitably.
It wasn't fair to sell a crippled version of the product at a price that we simply couldn't maintain.
We're going to stop selling the Home Edition altogether, and eat the cost of moving every existing Home Edition user to the Professional Edition.
I, for one, can't bear to hear my own voice so you'll have to watch it without me.
key_insight: A crippled cheap edition that frustrates users and can't sustain itself financially is worse than no cheap edition at all — better to simplify pricing and absorb the short-term cost.
